name: Node.js
description: A JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine for building fast, scalable server-side applications. Powers backend services, APIs, real-time apps, CLIs, and microservices.
overview: Node.js, created by Ryan Dahl in 2009, revolutionized JavaScript by bringing it to the server side. Built on Chrome's V8 JavaScript engine, Node uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model that makes it lightweight and efficient for data-intensive real-time applications. Its package ecosystem (npm) is the largest in the world with over 2 million packages. Node.js excels at building RESTful APIs, real-time services (WebSockets), CLI tools, and microservices. The introduction of ES modules, worker threads, and performance improvements in recent versions has solidified Node.js as a premier backend choice. Major companies like Netflix, LinkedIn, Uber, and PayPal run Node.js in production. Node.js is also the foundation for frontend tooling (Vite, Webpack, Babel) and desktop apps (Electron).

interviewQuestions.question: How does the Node.js event loop work?
interviewQuestions.answer: The event loop is a single-threaded loop that processes operations in phases: timers (setTimeout, setInterval), pending callbacks (I/O callbacks), idle/prepare, poll (retrieve new I/O events), check (setImmediate callbacks), close callbacks. Between each phase, the microtask queue (process.nextTick, Promise callbacks) is processed. This non-blocking model allows Node.js to handle thousands of concurrent connections with a single thread.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between process.nextTick and setImmediate?
interviewQuestions.answer: process.nextTick fires before the next event loop iteration (on the microtask queue, before any I/O). setImmediate fires during the check phase of the next iteration (after I/O). process.nextTick has higher priority but can lead to I/O starvation if called recursively. setImmediate is safer for deferring work and doesn't block I/O. In practice, prefer setImmediate unless you need nextTick's priority.

interviewQuestions.question: What are streams in Node.js? Explain the types.
interviewQuestions.answer: Streams are objects that let you read/write data continuously instead of loading everything into memory. Types: Readable (read data source), Writable (write data destination), Transform (modify data during read/write), Duplex (both readable and writable). Benefits: memory efficiency (process large files without loading entirely), time efficiency (start processing as data arrives). Pipes connect streams automatically with backpressure handling.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you handle errors in Express.js applications?
interviewQuestions.answer: Centralized error handler middleware (err, req, res, next) => {} catches all errors passed via next(err). Wrap async route handlers to catch promise rejections. Use a custom AppError class extending Error with statusCode. Differentiate: operational errors (handle gracefully) vs programmer errors (crash and restart). Use helmet for security headers, express-validator for input validation, and catch uncaughtException/unhandledRejection globally.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES Modules in Node.js?
interviewQuestions.answer: CommonJS: require/module.exports, synchronous, dynamic loading, available by default in .js files. ES Modules: import/export, static analysis, tree-shakable, top-level await. Use .mjs extension or type: 'module' in package.json for ESM. ESM has static imports (hoisted) and dynamic import(). CommonJS is still widely used but ESM is the future of Node.js module system.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the middleware pattern in Express.
interviewQuestions.answer: Middleware functions have access to req, res, and next. They execute in order, can modify req/res, end the request, or call next() to pass control. Types: application-level (app.use), router-level (router.use), error-handling (4 params), built-in (express.json, express.static), third-party (morgan, cors, helmet). Middleware execution order matters — place error handlers last.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you manage database connections in Node.js?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use connection pooling (pg.Pool for PostgreSQL, mongoose.createConnection for MongoDB). Pool benefits: reuses connections, limits concurrent connections, handles timeouts. Configure pool size based on CPU cores (typically pool size = CPU cores * 2 + overhead). Handle connection errors and reconnect logic. Use environment-specific pool settings. Never create/destroy connections per request.

interviewQuestions.question: What is clustering in Node.js and when to use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Cluster module creates child processes (workers) that share the same server port. Each worker is a separate Node.js process with its own event loop and memory. The master process distributes incoming connections among workers (round-robin on most platforms). Use clustering to utilize multi-core CPUs. PM2 cluster mode handles this automatically. Alternative: use worker threads for CPU-intensive tasks within a single process.

interviewQuestions.question: What are worker threads and how do they differ from clustering?
interviewQuestions.answer: Worker threads (Node.js 12+) run JavaScript in parallel within the same process, sharing memory via SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics. Unlike clustering (separate processes), worker threads share module loading and memory but have isolated V8 instances and event loops. Use workers for CPU-intensive tasks (image processing, data transformation) without the memory overhead of clustering.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you secure a Node.js production application?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use helmet middleware for security headers. Enable HTTPS. Use rate limiting (express-rate-limit). Validate/sanitize inputs (express-validator, Joi, Zod). Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection. Store secrets in environment variables. Use CORS appropriately. Set secure HTTP-only cookies. Use csurf for CSRF protection. Keep dependencies updated with npm audit. Use HSTS, CSP headers. Log security events.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the concept of backpressure in Node.js streams.
interviewQuestions.answer: Backpressure occurs when a writable stream receives data faster than it can process. Node.js's pipe() automatically handles backpressure — if the writable stream's internal buffer is full (highWaterMark), it returns false from write(), and the readable stream pauses. You can manually handle with drain event (emitted when buffer is ready). Without backpressure, data would overflow memory indefinitely.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you handle memory leaks in Node.js applications?
interviewQuestions.answer: Common causes: global variables, closure references, event listeners not removed, timers/intervals not cleared, large object references, forgetting to close connections. Detection: heap snapshots with --heap-prof, Chrome DevTools Memory tab, clinic.js. Monitoring: process.memoryUsage(), Prometheus metrics. Prevention: proper cleanup in events, use weak references (WeakRef), implement memory limits.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between npm and npx?
interviewQuestions.answer: npm installs packages globally or locally and runs scripts. npx executes packages without installing them permanently (downloads to cache and runs). npx is useful for: running one-off commands (create-react-app, prisma), trying packages without committing to installation, running specific package versions. npx is bundled with npm 5.2+.

bestPractices: Use asynchronous APIs (async/await) instead of synchronous methods — never block the event loop
Use environment variables for all configuration — never hardcode secrets in source code
Use a process manager (PM2) in production — handles restarts, logs, clustering
Always handle promise rejections — use process.on('unhandledRejection') and process.on('uncaughtException')
Use try/catch in all async route handlers — wrap with a catch-all async error handler
Use structured logging (Winston/Pino) with JSON format instead of console.log in production
Implement request validation at the edge (Joi/Zod) — validate all inputs before use
Use compression (compression middleware) for API responses and static assets
Use connection pooling for databases — optimize pool size based on workload
Use rate limiting and request size limits to protect against abuse (express-rate-limit, helmet)
Implement health check endpoints for monitoring and load balancer probes
Use npm audit and Snyk to detect vulnerable dependencies regularly
Use graceful shutdown — handle SIGTERM, close connections, finish pending requests
Use TypeScript for large projects — catches type errors at compile time
Write tests: unit tests (Jest) + integration tests (Supertest) + E2E tests (Playwright)
Use dependency injection for testable code — avoid tight coupling between modules
Keep your API stateless — use external stores (Redis) for session data
Monitor memory and CPU usage — set up alerts for anomalies
Avoid npm package bloat — prefer small, focused packages and audit bundle size

careerOpportunities.description: Build and maintain Node.js APIs, write tests, debug issues, and learn production practices.
Design and implement scalable backend services, databases, authentication, and API integrations.
Work across frontend (React) and backend (Node.js) to build end-to-end features.
Build distributed microservices using Node.js with message queues, containers, and orchestration.
Build internal tooling, CI/CD pipelines, monitoring, and deployment infrastructure for Node.js services.
Specialize in profiling, optimizing, and debugging Node.js applications for maximum performance.
